text: The surgeon identifies filmy adhesions in the surgical field and Rouvier's sulcus visible behind these adhesions.
The Rouvier sulcus is visible to the left of the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies and dissects the cystic artery and cystic duct, achieving the critical view of safety. The cystic artery is visible as it is being carefully dissected.
The surgeon alternates between immunofluorescence imaging and standard view, performing a straightforward dissection to achieve the critical view of safety, with the liver visible in the background.
The surgeon identifies the cystic duct, which is clearly visible as it connects directly to the gallbladder.
The surgeon places two clips on the stay side and one clip on the specimen side of the cystic duct, securing it for subsequent steps in the procedure.
The surgeon observes the cystic plate, noting its average width and the thinning at the top. The gallbladder is visible, with attention to avoiding inadvertent cholecystotomies.
